summary: Gruber identifies an inconsistency in how Google search results appear on the iPhone depending on whether users search via Safari's built-in toolbar or through google.com directly. The toolbar search returns a standard desktop-style results page that's nearly illegible on the iPhone screen, while searching through google.com delivers a properly optimized mobile layout. He questions why Google's iPhone-optimized mode isn't triggered for toolbar searches, noting it's unclear whether Apple or Google should fix this. He updates the post noting Google has acknowledged the issue and plans to expand the optimized format. He also compares Yahoo's iPhone-optimized search results unfavorably, finding them too large and inefficient with screen space.
key_points: iPhone's built-in Safari search field returns standard desktop Google results that are effectively illegible, while google.com serves properly optimized mobile results
The responsibility for fixing this falls ambiguously between Apple (changing the query string) and Google (detecting iPhone requests server-side)
Google acknowledged the issue and stated plans to expand iPhone-optimized results to other search entry points
Yahoo already serves iPhone-optimized results through Safari's toolbar but wastes screen space by making each result too large
Google's company-wide iPhone web optimization, including as-you-type suggestions, represents some of the best mobile web design available
money_quotes: In this initial state, the results are effectively illegible.
I don't understand why, if Google has an iPhone-optimized search results mode, this mode isn't used for queries initiated via the iPhone's built-in Google search field.

It's not clear to me whether Apple should change MobileSafari to send a different query string to Google to trigger these iPhone-optimized results, or whether Google should just handle it on their end.
Right idea, but I don't care for Yahoo's presentation. Each result is too big — you can only see the first two without scrolling, whereas with Google's iPhone display you can see four.
Google, company-wide, has made some of the best iPhone-optimized web sites I've seen.
key_insight: A simple UX inconsistency — the iPhone's built-in search field not triggering Google's own mobile-optimized results — reveals the ambiguous responsibility between platform owner and service provider in delivering a coherent mobile experience.
